skill.name: esm
skill.description: Toolkit for protein language models (ESM3 for multimodal generative protein design; ESM C for efficient embeddings). Use when you need sequence/structure/function generation or prediction, inverse folding, protein embeddings, or scalable inference via local weights or the Forge API.
skill.summary: Guides protein design and analysis with ESM3 and ESM C language models for structure prediction, inverse folding, and embeddings.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 304 static findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The skill is a documentation and reference package for protein language models (ESM3, ESM C). Detected patterns (weak crypto, eval, C2 keywords, shell execution) are all from legitimate ML code patterns in markdown examples: model.eval() is a PyTorch method, shell backticks are inside code blocks, and weak crypto flags are model name collisions (esmc-300m pattern matched crypto algorithm names). The only real risk factors are expected behaviors: references to the Forge API URL, environment variable access for API tokens, and PDB file writes in example code. This skill is safe to publish as a reference guide.

low_findings.title: Environment variable access for API authentication
low_findings.description: SKILL.md:79 uses os.environ.get('FORGE_TOKEN') to read an API token. This is standard practice for authenticating with the EvolutionAI Forge API. No credential exfiltration risk - the token is used only for the documented protein inference API endpoint.

low_findings.title: Network requests to external API
low_findings.description: References to Forge API URL (forge.evolutionaryscale.ai) and GitHub source repository. These are legitimate endpoints for the documented protein language model service. No data exfiltration or unauthorized network activity is present.

content.user_title: Design and Analyze Proteins with ESM Language Models
content.value_statement: Protein engineering with language models requires deep knowledge of model APIs and generation parameters. This skill provides structured guidance for using ESM3 and ESM C to generate protein sequences, predict structures, and compute embeddings.
actual_capabilities: Generate new protein sequences conditioned on structure or function annotations using ESM3
Complete masked residues in existing protein sequences for variant design
Predict 3D protein structure from amino acid sequence using ESM3 structure track
Perform inverse folding to design sequences compatible with a target structure
Compute protein embeddings for downstream ML tasks using ESM C models
Execute batch inference via the Forge API for high-throughput workloads
limitations: Does not run ESM models directly; provides reference guidance for the user to execute
Output quality depends on the chosen model size and generation parameters
Forge API requires a valid authentication token and internet access
Structure prediction accuracy varies for unusual or engineered protein sequences

use_cases.title: Novel Enzyme Design
use_cases.description: A researcher wants to design a new enzyme with specific catalytic properties. Use ESM3 to generate sequences conditioned on desired function annotations and structure constraints.

use_cases.title: Protein Variant Screening
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics scientist needs to generate and evaluate hundreds of protein variants. Use ESM C embeddings combined with ESM3 generation to create and cluster variant sequences for experimental testing.

use_cases.title: Structure-Guided Protein Design
use_cases.description: A computational biologist has a target protein structure and needs sequences that fold into that shape. Use ESM3 inverse folding to design sequences compatible with the given backbone coordinates.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Sequence Completion
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ESM skill to complete a protein sequence. I have a partial sequence: MPRT___KEND. Generate the complete sequence using ESM3 with the sequence track and 8 generation steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user learning basic ESM3 sequence generation

prompt_templates.title: Batch Variant Generation via Forge
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ESM skill to generate 10 variants of the sequence MPRTKEINDAGLIVHSP. Use the Forge API with async batch processing and set temperature to 0.7 for balanced diversity. Show the generation parameters and expected outputs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher generating multiple variants for screening

prompt_templates.title: Structure Prediction from Sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ESM skill to predict the 3D structure of the sequence MPRTKEINDAGLIVHSP. Use ESM3 with the structure track. Explain how to export the results to PDB format and visualize the predicted coordinates.

prompt_templates.title: Inverse Folding for Target Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ESM skill to design a protein sequence for a given PDB structure. Load the structure from a PDB file, remove the existing sequence, and generate a new compatible sequence using ESM3 inverse folding on the sequence track.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user designing sequences for a known structure

output_examples.input: Complete the masked protein sequence ACD___EFG using ESM3 local model
output_examples.output: Local ESM3 model generated the completed sequence. The masked positions (_ _ _) were filled with residues K, L, and V respectively. Full sequence: ACDKLVEFG. Generation used sequence track with 3 steps and default temperature.

output_examples.input: Compare ESM C and ESM2 for embedding quality on a set of kinase sequences
output_examples.output: ESM C 300M generated embeddings 3x faster than ESM2-650M with improved representation quality. For batch embedding of 100 kinase sequences, ESM C completed in approximately 15 seconds on a consumer GPU. Embedding dimensions depend on model size: 1152 for esmc-600m.

best_practices: Use async batch generation with the Forge API when processing many sequences to maximize throughput
Match the number of generation steps to the number of masked residues for optimal sequence completion results
Test with smaller model sizes first (esmc-300m) before scaling to larger models for production workloads
anti_patterns: Do not use maximum temperature (1.0) for initial designs; start with 0.5-0.7 and adjust based on result quality
Do not send full protein sequences without masking when you want partial sequence completion
Do not skip environment variable setup for Forge API tokens; the client will fail without authentication

faq.question: What is the difference between ESM3 and ESM C?
faq.answer: ESM3 is a multimodal model that generates across sequence, structure, and function tracks for protein design. ESM C is optimized for fast, high-quality embeddings and representation learning.

faq.question: Do I need a GPU to use this skill?
faq.answer: Local inference with ESM models requires a GPU for practical performance. You can use the Forge API for cloud-based inference without local GPU hardware.

faq.question: How do I get a Forge API token?
faq.answer: Register with the EvolutionAI Forge platform to obtain an authentication token. Set it as the FORGE_TOKEN environment variable before using Forge-based features.

faq.question: Can this skill predict protein function from sequence alone?
faq.answer: ESM3 can generate functional annotations conditioned on sequence, and ESM C embeddings can be used with downstream classifiers for function prediction.

faq.question: What model sizes are available?
faq.answer: ESM C offers 300M, 600M, and 6B parameter models. ESM3 offers small (open), medium, and large model sizes available through the Forge API.

faq.question: Does this skill run code automatically?
faq.answer: No. This is a reference and guidance skill. The AI provides instructions and example code, but you must execute the commands in your own environment.
